title: Hávamál
draftNote: Gestaþáttr, the opening gnomic section (stanzas 1–79). Being prepared and published in batches from Neckel 1914 — stanzas 1–60 are done so far; the rest are on the way.
source: Gustav Neckel, Edda: Die Lieder des Codex Regius nebst verwandten Denkmälern, 1st edition (Heidelberg: Carl Winter, 1914), Hávamál, stanzas 1–79 (the Gestaþáttr), pp. 16–29. Text transcribed from Neckel's page images and reproduced in his normalization exactly — þ, ð, æ, œ, the nasal vowel ǫ, ø, acute length-marks, the -z/-az/-oz middle endings, i for j (sitia, miǫk), -om datives, þvíat as one word. Italicized (editorially emended or expanded) letters are set as plain letters; major emendations are noted. Eddic poems are cited by stanza, so the margin numbers are stanza numbers. Pronunciation convention: reconstructed classical Old Icelandic, c. 1200. Translations made fresh from the original.
about: The Hávamál (‘Sayings of the High One’) survives in a single medieval manuscript, the Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to, c. 1270); everything printed here is a modern editor's normalization of that one source, and the standard spelling itself is a scholarly convention, not something the scribe wrote consistently. The Gestaþáttr — ‘the guest's section’ — is the first and most famous part: loosely linked proverbs of hospitality, caution, drink, friendship, and conduct, voiced as if by Odin. The verse is ljóðaháttr, the chant-meter of Norse wisdom poetry. The numbers in the margin are stanza numbers, the usual way of citing Eddic verse.

sentences.t:
Gáttir allar,
áðr gangi fram,
um skoðaz skyli,
um skygnaz skyli;
þvíat óvíst er at vita,
hvar óvinir sitia á fleti fyrir.
sentences.l:
All the doorways,
before one walks forward,
should be scanned,
should be spied out;
for it is uncertain to know
where enemies sit on the hall-floor ahead.
sentences.i: Before you step through any doorway, look it all over, scout it well — for you can never know for certain where enemies may be sitting in the hall ahead of you.
sentences.n: The poem opens in ljóðaháttr, the ‘chant meter’ of Old Norse wisdom verse: paired short lines whose stressed syllables alliterate (Gáttir–gangi; skoðaz–skygnaz). skyli ‘should’ is the gnomic subjunctive of skulu — it gives proverbs their timeless ‘one should…’. skoðaz / skygnaz end in the -z middle voice (worn down from -sk ‘oneself’), roughly ‘be scanned / peer about’. um before a verb is an old, now-empty particle — ignore it in the sense. flet (dat. fleti) is the raised plank-floor along a hall's walls where people sat. Neckel prints this text in an older normalized spelling (þvíat one word, sitia for sitja, -z endings); we follow his 1914 edition exactly.

sentences.t:
Gefendr heilir!
Gestr er inn kominn:
hvar skal sitia siá?
miǫk er bráðr,
sá er at brǫndom skal
síns um freista frama.
sentences.l:
Givers, hail!
A guest is come in:
where shall this one sit?
Very hasty is he,
who at the fire-logs must
make trial of his own worth.
sentences.i: Good health to the givers! A guest has stepped inside — where is he to sit? The man who must prove his worth at the hearth-fire stands there in an anxious hurry.
sentences.n: A greeting to the hosts: Gefendr ‘the givers’ (of hospitality), heilir ‘hale, whole’ — the ancestor of English hail and wassail. inn is the adverb ‘in’ (gestr er inn kominn, ‘a guest has come in’); siá is the demonstrative ‘this one’ (nom.), not to be confused with sá ‘that one, he’. The letter ǫ in miǫk and brǫndom is Neckel's spelling of a nasal o-vowel (later Icelandic ö); use the ‘þ→th’ button to fold it to a plain o. The second half is debated: at brǫndom is literally ‘at the fire-logs’ (the hearth), and síns um freista frama ‘to make trial of his own worth’ — we keep the literal image and flag the doubt.

sentences.t:
Elz er þǫrf,
þeims inn er kominn
ok á kné kalinn;
matar ok váða
er manni þǫrf,
þeim er hefir um fiall farit.
sentences.l:
Of fire there is need,
for him who is come in
and is chilled to the knee;
of food and clothing
has a man need,
he who has fared over the fell.
sentences.i: Fire is what a man needs when he comes in numb with cold to the knee; food and dry clothes he needs, after crossing the mountains.
sentences.n: Elz = eld(s), genitive of eldr ‘fire’, governed by þǫrf ‘need’ (er þǫrf + genitive = ‘there is need of’). þeims = þeim es, ‘for him who’. um fiall farit: to have travelled across the highland — cold, wet, and hungry on arrival.

sentences.t:
Vatz er þǫrf,
þeim er til verðar kømr,
þerro ok þióðlaðar,
góðs um œðis,
ef sér geta mætti,
orz ok endrþǫgo.
sentences.i: A guest coming to a meal needs water, a towel, and a friendly welcome — a kind manner, if he can find it, a fair hearing and, in his turn, a courteous silence.
sentences.n: A quietly interpretive stanza. þióðlaðar (gen. of þióðlǫð) = a hearty invitation/welcome. góðs um œðis: ‘of good disposition’ (œði ‘temper, manner’). orz ok endrþǫgo: ‘of speech and of silence-in-return’ — the guest should be both heard and, when it is his turn to listen, silent.

sentences.i: A man who travels far needs his wits about him; at home anything passes, but the man who knows nothing and sits among the clever becomes a laughing-stock.
sentences.n: dælt (from dæll) ‘easy, undemanding’: heima hvat ‘anything at home’ — among your own, ignorance is not exposed. at augabragði verðr = ‘becomes (an object) of a wink/mockery’, i.e. a laughing-stock. með snotrom ‘among the wise’ (dat. pl. of snotr).

sentences.t:
At hyggiandi sinni
skylit maðr hrœsinn vera,
heldr gætinn at geði;
þá er horskr ok þǫgull
kømr heimisgarða til,
sialdan verðr víti vǫrom;
þvíat óbrigðra vin
fær maðr aldregi
en manvit mikit.
sentences.l:
Of his understanding
a man should not be boastful,
but rather wary in mind;
when a wise and silent man
comes to a homestead,
harm seldom befalls the wary;
for a more faithful friend
a man never gets
than much good sense.
sentences.i: A man should not flaunt his cleverness, but keep it guarded; when a shrewd, quiet man comes to a house, misfortune rarely touches the careful — for no friend is more dependable than a good store of common sense.
sentences.n: An expanded stanza (nine lines). skylit = skyli-at, ‘should not’. hrœsinn ‘boastful’; gætinn at geði ‘guarded in mind’. vǫrom (dat. pl. of varr ‘wary’). The closing couplet is proverbial: manvit ‘mother-wit, common sense’ is the truest friend.

sentences.t:
Inn vari gestr,
er til verðar kømr,
þunno hlióði þegir,
eyrom hlýðir,
en augom skoðar;
svá nýsiz fróðra hverr fyrir.
sentences.l:
The wary guest,
who comes to a meal,
keeps silent with taut hearing,
listens with his ears,
and looks about with his eyes;
so every wise man scouts ahead.
sentences.i: A cautious guest at a meal keeps quiet, listening keenly and watching closely — that is how every prudent man feels his way.
sentences.n: þunno hlióði þegir: ‘is silent with thin/taut hearing’ — alert, straining to catch everything. nýsiz … fyrir ‘scouts ahead, feels the ground’ (middle voice of nýsa). Echoes the caution of stanza 1.

sentences.t:
Hinn er sæll,
er sér um getr
lof ok líknstafi;
ódælla er við þat,
er maðr eiga skal
annars brióstom í.
sentences.l:
He is fortunate,
who gets for himself
praise and words of favour;
far harder it is
with what a man must own
inside another's breast.
sentences.i: Fortunate is the man who wins praise and goodwill by his own doing; it is a chancier thing to depend on what lives inside someone else's heart.
sentences.n: líknstafi (acc. pl. of líknstafr) ‘words/tokens of favour, kindly regard’. ódælla ‘less easy, harder’ (comparative of ódæll). The point: esteem you earn yourself is secure; what you must keep in another's breast (their goodwill) is not in your control.

sentences.t:
Sá er sæll,
er siálfr um á
lof ok vit, meðan lifir;
þvíat ill ráð
hefir maðr opt þegit
annars brióstom ór.
sentences.l:
He is fortunate,
who for himself has
praise and wit, while he lives;
for ill counsel
a man has often received
out of another's breast.
sentences.i: Happy the man who owns praise and good sense of his own while he lives — for many a man has taken bad advice out of someone else's heart.
sentences.n: A companion to stanza 8: better to carry your own judgement (vit) than to draw counsel from another's breast, which is often ill (ill ráð).

sentences.t:
Byrði betri
berrat maðr brauto at,
en sé manvit mikit;
auði betra
þikkir þat í ókunnom stað,
slíkt er válaðs vera.
sentences.l:
A better burden
a man bears not on the road
than much good sense;
better than riches
it seems in an unknown place,
such is the refuge of the destitute.
sentences.i: No better load can a man carry on his travels than plenty of common sense; in a strange place it is worth more than wealth — it is the poor man's refuge.
sentences.n: berrat = berr-at ‘bears not’. brauto at (dat. of braut ‘road’) ‘on the way’. auði betra ‘better than wealth’ (dat. of auðr). válaðs vera: ‘the being/refuge of the destitute’ (válaðr ‘poor, wretched’) — sense is the one wealth the poor can rely on.

sentences.t:
Byrði betri
berrat maðr brauto at,
en sé manvit mikit;
vegnest verra
vegra hann velli at,
en sé ofdrykkia ǫls.
sentences.l:
A better burden
a man bears not on the road
than much good sense;
a worse provision
he carries not over the field
than too much ale-drinking.
sentences.i: No better load can a man carry on his travels than plenty of common sense; and no worse road-provision than drinking too much ale.
sentences.n: The first half repeats stanza 10 word for word — a deliberate ‘tvískelfing’ (twinned opening) pivoting to a new point. vegnest ‘journey-provisions’ (way-food). ofdrykkia ǫls ‘over-drinking of ale’ (ǫls, gen. of ǫl) — the theme of the next stanzas.

sentences.t:
Era svá gott,
sem gott kveða,
ǫl alda sonom;
þvíat færa veit,
er fleira drekkr,
síns til geðs gumi.
sentences.i: Ale is not the good thing for men that it is made out to be; for the more a man drinks, the less he keeps of his own wits.
sentences.n: Era = er-a ‘is not’. alda sonom ‘for the sons of men’ (poetic ‘for men’). The last three lines are compressed: ‘the less (a man) knows of his own mind (geð), the more he drinks’ — drink and self-command run opposite. færa … fleira: comparatives ‘the less … the more’.

sentences.t:
Óminnis hegri heitir,
sá er yfir ǫlðrom þrumir:
hann stelr geði guma;
þess fugls fiǫðrom
ek fiǫtraðr vark
í garði Gunnlaðar.
sentences.l:
The heron of forgetfulness it is called,
the one that broods over ale-feasts:
it steals a man's mind;
with that bird's feathers
I was fettered
in Gunnlǫð's court.
sentences.i: There is a bird men call the heron of oblivion, that hovers over the drinking; it steals a man's wits away. By its feathers I too was shackled, in Gunnlǫð's dwelling.
sentences.n: Óminnis hegri ‘heron of forgetfulness’ — a striking kenning for drunkenness, imagined as a bird brooding over the ǫlðr (ale-feast). The speaker is now Odin: Gunnlǫð is the giantess who guarded the mead of poetry, which Odin won (see the Rúnatal later). ek … vark ‘I was’ (vark, old 1st sg. past of vera). fiǫtraðr ‘fettered’.

sentences.t:
Ǫlr ek varð,
varð ofrǫlvi
at ins fróða Fialars;
því er ǫlðr batst,
at aptr of heimtir
hverr sitt geð gumi.
sentences.l:
Drunk I became,
I became dead-drunk
at wise Fialarr's;
that is the best of ale-feasts,
when each man gets back
his own mind afterward.
sentences.i: Drunk I grew, blind drunk, at wise Fialar's; but the best of any ale-feast is the one a man comes away from with his wits still his own.
sentences.n: Odin's confession continues. ofrǫlvi ‘over-ale-ish, dead drunk’ (of- intensive + ǫlvi from ǫlr ‘drunk’). Fialarr: a name linked to the mead-of-poetry story. ǫlðr batst ‘the best (of) ale-feast’ (batst = superlative of vel/góðr). aptr of heimtir … sitt geð: ‘gets back his own mind’ — the moral turn after the boast.

sentences.t:
Þagalt ok hugalt
skyli þióðans barn
ok vígdiarft vera;
glaðr ok reifr
skyli gumna hverr,
unz sinn bíðr bana.
sentences.l:
Reserved and thoughtful
should a prince's child be
and bold in war;
glad and cheerful
should every man be,
until he awaits his death.
sentences.i: A king's son should be discreet, thoughtful, and brave in battle; and every man should be glad and cheerful until the day he meets his death.
sentences.n: Dagalt: ‘reserved, sparing of speech’ (a rare word; the sense ‘silent/discreet’). vígdiarft ‘war-bold’. þióðans barn ‘a prince's child’ (þióðann ‘king, lord’). unz sinn bíðr bana ‘until he awaits his death’ — face life cheerfully to the end.

sentences.t:
Ósniallr maðr
hyggz muno ey lifa,
ef hann við víg varaz;
en elli gefr
hánom engi frið,
þótt hánom geirar gefi.
sentences.l:
The cowardly man
thinks he will live forever,
if he keeps away from war;
but old age gives him
no peace,
though spears may spare him.
sentences.i: The coward imagines he will live forever if only he shuns battle — but old age grants him no truce, even where spears let him be.
sentences.n: Ósniallr ‘un-valiant, cowardly’. hyggz … muno ey lifa ‘thinks he will live forever’ (ey ‘ever’). The point cuts against caution taken too far: you cannot dodge death by dodging danger, for elli ‘old age’ comes regardless.

sentences.t:
Kópir afglapi,
er til kynnis kømr,
þylsk hann um eða þrumir;
allt er senn,
ef hann sylg um getr:
uppi er þá geð guma.
sentences.i: The fool gawps when he comes as a guest, muttering under his breath or sitting mute; but the moment he gets a drink, out it all comes — his whole mind is on show.
sentences.n: Kópir ‘gapes, stares stupidly’; afglapi ‘fool, dolt’. þylsk … um ‘mumbles to himself’ (middle of þylja); þrumir ‘sits torpid, broods’. uppi er … geð ‘the mind is up/out, exposed’ — drink undoes the fool who cannot hold his tongue.

sentences.t:
Sá einn veit,
er víða ratar
ok hefir fiǫlð um farit,
hverio geði
stýrir gumna hverr,
sá er vitandi er vits.
sentences.l:
He alone knows,
who travels widely
and has fared far and wide,
by what disposition
every man is governed —
he who has understanding of his wits.
sentences.i: Only the man who has travelled widely and seen much truly knows what temper rules each person he meets — the man, that is, who has his wits about him.
sentences.n: A counterweight to stanza 5: wide travel, joined to sense, teaches how to read people. hverio geði … stýrir ‘by what disposition (each) is steered’. sá er vitandi er vits ‘he who is knowing of wit’ — travel only teaches the already-shrewd.

sentences.t:
Haldit maðr á keri,
drekki þó at hófi miǫð,
mæli þarft eða þegi;
ókynnis þess
vár þik engi maðr,
at þu gangir snemma at sofa.
sentences.l:
Let a man not cling to the cup,
yet let him drink mead in measure,
let him speak what is needful or be silent;
of discourtesy in this
no man will blame you,
that you go early to sleep.
sentences.i: Don't hog the cup — but drink your share of the mead; say what needs saying or hold your peace. No one will fault you for bad manners if you go to bed early.
sentences.n: Haldit = haldi-at ‘let (him) not hold on’. at hófi ‘in moderation’ (hóf ‘measure’). mæli þarft eða þegi ‘let him speak what is useful or be silent’. vár … engi maðr ‘no man will reproach/accuse’ (vá ‘to blame’); ókynnis (gen.) ‘for discourtesy’. Leaving early sober is no rudeness.

sentences.t:
Gráðugr halr,
nema geðs viti,
etr sér aldrtrega;
opt fær hlœgis,
er með horskom kømr,
manni heimskom magi.
sentences.l:
The greedy man,
unless he minds his sense,
eats himself into lifelong grief;
often his belly
brings a wise man's laughter
upon the foolish man, when he comes among the shrewd.
sentences.i: The greedy man, if he doesn't watch himself, eats his way into lasting misery; among sensible folk, a fool's belly often makes him a laughing-stock.
sentences.n: Gráðugr ‘greedy’; halr ‘man’. aldrtrega ‘lifelong grief’ (aldr ‘life’ + tregi ‘grief’). fær hlœgis ‘gets (cause for) laughter’, i.e. earns ridicule; magi ‘belly, stomach’ is the subject that shames the heimskr (foolish) man among the horskir (wise). Gluttony, like drink, exposes lack of self-command.

sentences.t:
Hiarðir þat vito,
nær þær heim skolo,
ok ganga þá af grasi;
en ósviðr maðr
kann ævagi
síns um mál maga.
sentences.l:
Herds know that,
when they must go home,
and then leave the grass;
but the foolish man
never knows
the measure of his own belly.
sentences.i: Cattle know when it is time to go home and quit the pasture; but a foolish man never knows the measure of his own stomach.
sentences.n: A jab at the glutton of st. 20: even beasts have the sense he lacks. mál maga 'the measure of the belly' (mál 'measure' + magi). ævagi 'never ever' (æva + emphatic -gi). ósviðr 'unwise'.

sentences.t:
Vesall maðr
ok illa skapi
hlær at hvívetna;
hitki hann veit,
er hann vita þyrpti,
at hann era vamma vanr.
sentences.l:
A wretched man
and of ill temper
laughs at everything;
this he does not know,
which he ought to know,
that he is not free of faults.
sentences.i: A miserable, ill-natured man laughs at everything; the one thing he fails to grasp — though he ought to — is that he is not without faults himself.
sentences.n: illa skapi 'ill-disposed, of bad temper' (dat.). hlær at hvívetna 'laughs at everything' (hvívetna, dat. of hvatvetna). hitki … er 'not that … which' (hit-gi 'that-not'). vamma vanr 'lacking in faults'; era vanr 'is not lacking' = is not free of them.

sentences.t:
Ósviðr maðr
vakir um allar nætr
ok hyggr at hvívetna;
þá er móðr,
er at morni kømr:
allt er víl, sem var.
sentences.l:
The unwise man
lies awake all nights
and broods over everything;
then he is weary,
when morning comes:
all is woe, as it was.
sentences.i: The foolish man lies awake night after night, fretting over everything; and when morning comes he is worn out — and every trouble is exactly where it was.
sentences.n: vakir 'is awake'; hyggr at 'broods over'. móðr 'weary, spent'. víl 'misery, woe'. The point: worry costs sleep and changes nothing.

sentences.t:
Ósnotr maðr
hyggr sér alla vera
viðhlæiendr vini;
hitki hann fiðr,
þótt þeir um hann fár lesi,
ef hann með snotrom sitr.
sentences.l:
The foolish man
thinks all are, for him,
laughing companions, friends;
he does not notice,
though they pick out faults about him,
when he sits among the wise.
sentences.i: The foolish man imagines that everyone who laughs along with him is a friend; he never notices, even when they run him down, when he is sitting among clever folk.
sentences.n: viðhlæiendr 'those who laugh with one', i.e. flatterers (pres. part.). fiðr 'notices' (finna). fár lesi 'pick out malice' (fár 'harm, spite'; lesa 'to glean'). The fool cannot tell flattery from friendship.

sentences.t:
Ósnotr maðr
hyggr sér alla vera
viðhlæiendr vini;
þá þat finnr,
er at þingi kømr,
at hann á formælendr fá.
sentences.l:
The foolish man
thinks all are, for him,
laughing companions, friends;
then he finds this,
when he comes to the Thing,
that he has few to speak for him.
sentences.i: The foolish man thinks everyone who laughs with him is his friend — then finds, when he comes to the assembly, that he has few to plead his cause.
sentences.n: A twin of st. 24, pivoting to the test. þingi (dat. of þing) 'assembly, court'. formælendr 'advocates, spokesmen'. á … fá 'has few'. When it matters, flatterers are gone.

sentences.t:
Ósnotr maðr
þikkiz allt vita,
ef hann á sér í vá vero;
hitki hann veit,
hvat hann skal við kveða,
ef hans freista firar.
sentences.l:
The foolish man
thinks he knows everything,
if he has a nook to himself;
this he does not know,
what he shall reply,
if men put him to the test.
sentences.i: The foolish man fancies he knows it all, safe in his own corner; but he has no idea what to say when people put him to the test.
sentences.n: þikkiz 'imagines' (middle). á sér í vá vero 'has a nook to stay in' (vá 'corner, recess'; vero 'a being, staying') — the reading of this line is uncertain. við kveða 'answer, reply'. freista firar 'men test him' (firar 'men').

sentences.t:
Ósnotr maðr,
er með aldir kømr,
þat er batst, at hann þegi;
engi þat veit,
at hann ekki kann,
nema hann mæli til mart.
sentences.l:
The foolish man,
when he comes among people,
it is best that he be silent;
no one knows this,
that he knows nothing,
unless he talks too much.
sentences.i: When a foolish man comes among others, the best thing he can do is keep quiet; no one will know he knows nothing — unless he talks too much.
sentences.n: A famous proverb. með aldir 'among men' (aldir 'people'). batst 'best'. mæli til mart 'speaks too much' (til mart 'too much'). Silence hides ignorance; chatter exposes it.

sentences.t:
Fróðr sá þykkiz,
er fregna kann
ok segia it sama;
eyvito leyna
mego ýta sønir,
því er gengr um guma.
sentences.l:
He is thought wise,
who can ask questions
and likewise tell;
nothing can they hide,
the sons of men,
of what goes round among people.
sentences.i: The man who can both ask and answer is reckoned wise; men can keep nothing hidden of what is said about people.
sentences.n: fregna 'to inquire'; segia it sama 'tell likewise'. eyvito 'nothing at all' (acc.). ýta sønir 'the sons of men' (ýtar 'men'; sønir = synir). því er gengr um guma 'of what goes about among men' — gossip travels.

sentences.t:
Œrna mælir,
sá er æva þegir,
staðlauso stafi;
hraðmælt tunga,
nema haldendr eigi,
opt sér ógott um gelr.
sentences.l:
He speaks in plenty,
he who is never silent,
groundless words;
a fast-talking tongue,
unless it has restrainers,
often sings itself no good.
sentences.i: The man who is never silent talks far too much, and to no purpose; a hasty tongue, unless something holds it back, often sings its owner into trouble.
sentences.n: Œrna 'abundantly, more than enough' (adv.). æva þegir 'never is silent'. staðlauso stafi 'groundless staves/words' (staðlauss 'without foundation'; stafir 'words'). haldendr 'restrainers'. sér ógott um gelr 'crows no good for itself' (gala 'to chant, crow').

sentences.t:
At augabragði
skala maðr annan hafa,
þótt til kynnis komi;
margr þá fróðr þikkiz,
ef hann freginn erat
ok nái hann þurrfiallr þruma.
sentences.l:
To a mockery
a man should not hold another,
though he come visiting;
many a one then seems wise,
if he is not questioned
and gets to sit dry-skinned in quiet.
sentences.i: No man should make a mockery of another guest who comes to visit; many a man passes for wise so long as no one questions him and he can sit undisturbed and dry.
sentences.n: At augabragði … hafa 'hold up to ridicule' (cf. st. 5). skala 'should not' (skal + -a). freginn erat 'is not questioned' (erat = er + -at). þurrfiallr 'dry-skinned' — snug, unexposed. þruma 'sit still, keep quiet'.

sentences.t:
Fróðr þikkiz,
sá er flótta tekr,
gestr at gest hæðinn;
veita gǫrla,
sá er um verði glissir,
þótt hann með grǫmom glami.
sentences.l:
He thinks himself wise,
he who takes to flight,
a guest mocking a guest;
he does not know clearly,
he who sneers over the meal,
that he chatters among the hostile.
sentences.i: The guest who jeers at another guest and then slips away thinks himself clever; but the man who sneers over dinner little knows he is baiting men who mean him ill.
sentences.n: flótta tekr 'takes flight' after mocking. gestr at gest hæðinn 'a guest scoffing at a guest'. glissir 'grins, sneers' (um verði 'over the meal'). grǫmom 'the hostile' (dat. pl. of gramr). glami 'makes noise, chatters'. The reading is somewhat uncertain.

sentences.t:
Gumnar margir
erosk gagnhollir,
en at virði vrekaz;
aldar róg
þat mun æ vera:
órir gestr við gest.
sentences.l:
Many men
are kind to each other,
yet clash at the feast;
the strife of men
that will ever be:
guest wrangles with guest.
sentences.i: Many men are friendly enough to each other and yet quarrel at table; it will always be men's way to fall out — guest against guest.
sentences.n: erosk gagnhollir 'are mutually well-disposed' (gagnhollr; erosk = ero + reciprocal -sk). at virði vrekaz 'come to blows over the meal' (virði 'meal'; vrekaz 'clash'). aldar róg 'the strife of men'. órir 'quarrels' (reading uncertain).

sentences.t:
Árliga verðar
skyli maðr opt fáa,
nema til kynnis komi:
sitr ok snópir,
lætr, sem sólginn sé,
ok kann fregna at fá.
sentences.l:
Early meals
a man should often take,
unless he come visiting:
he sits and mopes,
behaves as if starved,
and can inquire of little.
sentences.i: A man should usually eat before he sets out — unless he's going as a guest; otherwise he sits there moping, acting half-starved, and can barely ask a question.
sentences.n: Árliga verðar 'early meals' (acc. pl.). snópir 'mopes, hangs about hungry'. lætr sem sólginn sé 'behaves as if famished' (sólginn 'ravenous'). fregna at fá 'get to ask (little)'. Eat first, so hunger doesn't shame you.

sentences.t:
Afhvarf mikit
er til illz vinar,
þótt á brauto búi;
en til góðs vinar
liggia gagnvegir,
þótt hann sé firr farinn.
sentences.l:
A great detour
it is to a bad friend,
though he dwell on the road;
but to a good friend
short-cuts lie,
though he be gone far away.
sentences.i: Getting to a false friend is a long way round, even if he lives right on your road; but to a true friend the ways run straight, however far off he has gone.
sentences.n: Afhvarf 'a turning-aside, detour'. til illz vinar 'to a bad friend' (illz, gen. of illr). gagnvegir 'direct ways, short-cuts'. firr farinn 'gone farther off'. A warm proverb: real friendship shortens the distance.

sentences.t:
Ganga skal,
skala gestr vera
ey í einom stað;
liúfr verðr leiðr,
ef lengi sitr
annars fletiom á.
sentences.i: It's time to move on — a guest should not stay forever in one spot; even a welcome man turns tiresome if he lingers too long on another's benches.
sentences.n: Ganga skal 'one must go'. skala … vera 'should not be'. ey í einom stað 'ever in one place'. liúfr verðr leiðr 'the dear becomes loathsome' — a proverb on outstaying one's welcome. fletiom (dat. pl. of flet) 'benches'.

sentences.t:
Bú er betra,
þótt lítit sé:
halr er heima hverr;
þótt tvær geitr eigi
ok taugreptan sal:
þat er þó betra en bœn.
sentences.l:
A household is better,
though it be little:
a man is master at home;
though he own two goats
and a cord-raftered hall:
that is yet better than begging.
sentences.i: A home of your own is better, however small; a man is his own master under his own roof. Even if you have only two goats and a barn roofed with twigs, that beats begging.
sentences.n: Bú 'household, farm'. halr er heima hverr 'every man is a lord at home'. taugreptan sal 'a hall raftered with cord/withies' — humble building. bœn 'a begging, boon-asking'. Independence over dependence.

sentences.t:
Bú er betra,
þótt lítit sé:
halr er heima hverr;
blóðugt er hiarta,
þeim er biðia skal
sér í mál hvert matar.
sentences.l:
A household is better,
though it be little:
a man is master at home;
bloody is the heart
of him who must beg
food for himself at every meal.
sentences.i: A home of your own is better, however small; a man is his own master at home. It bleeds the heart to have to beg your food at every single meal.
sentences.n: A twin of st. 36. blóðugt … hiarta 'a bleeding heart' — the shame of begging. biðia … matar 'beg for food' (biðja + gen.). í mál hvert 'at every mealtime' (mál 'meal(-time)').

sentences.t:
Vápnom sínom
skala maðr velli á
feti ganga framarr;
þvíat óvíst er at vita,
nær verðr á vegom úti
geirs um þǫrf guma.
sentences.l:
From his weapons
a man should not, on the field,
step a pace further off;
for it is uncertain to know
when, out on the roads,
a man will have need of a spear.
sentences.i: A man should never move a single step from his weapons out in the open, for there's no telling when, on the road, he may suddenly need a spear.
sentences.n: Vápnom sínom 'from his weapons' (dat. of separation). skala … framarr 'should not (go) further'. feti (dat. of fet) 'by a pace'. geirs um þǫrf 'need of a spear' (geirr; þǫrf + gen.). The world is dangerous — be armed (cf. st. 1).

sentences.t:
Fannka ek mildan mann
eða svá matar góðan,
at ei væri þiggia þegit,
eða síns féar
svági . . . . .,
at leið sé laun, ef þægi.
sentences.l:
I found no generous man,
nor one so free with food,
that a gift was not welcome;
nor of his own wealth
so . . . . .,
that a reward were hateful, if he took one.
sentences.i: I never met a man so open-handed or so free with his food that a gift would be unwelcome to him — nor one so [indifferent] to his wealth that a return-gift was unwelcome, when he had taken one.
sentences.n: Fannka 'I found not' (fann + -ka). at ei væri þiggia þegit 'that receiving was not received/welcome' — everyone likes to be given to. Neckel prints a lacuna (. . . . .) in line 5 where the manuscript fails. leið sé laun 'that a reward be hateful' — a return-gift is welcome too. The stanza is textually defective; we keep the gap visible.

sentences.t:
Fiár síns,
er fengit hefr,
skylit maðr þǫrf þola:
opt sparir leiðom,
þats hefir liúfom hugat;
mart gengr verr, en varir.
sentences.l:
Of the wealth,
which he has got,
a man should not endure want:
often one spares for the loathed
what one has meant for the dear;
much goes worse than one expects.
sentences.i: A man should not go without, of the wealth he has won: too often we hoard for someone we'd hate to give it to what we meant for those we love — many things turn out worse than expected.
sentences.n: Fiár síns 'of his property' (gen.). skylit … þǫrf þola 'should not suffer need' — don't stint yourself. sparir leiðom … liúfom hugat 'saves for the hated what was meant for the dear' (leiðr 'hateful'; liúfr 'dear'; hugat 'intended'). mart gengr verr en varir 'much goes worse than one expects' — misplaced thrift.

sentences.t:
Vápnom ok váðom
skolo vinir gleðiaz,
þat er á siálfom sýnst;
viðrgefendr ok endrgefendr
erosk lengst vinir,
ef þat bíðr at verða vel.
sentences.i: Friends should please each other with gifts of weapons and clothes — you can see it best worn on a man himself; those who give and give back in turn stay friends the longest, if the friendship is destined to prosper.
sentences.n: gleðiaz 'gladden one another' (middle). á siálfom sýnst 'most visible on oneself' — a friend's gifts show when worn. viðrgefendr ok endrgefendr 'those who give again and repay' — mutual gift-exchange keeps friendship alive. ef þat bíðr at verða vel 'if it is fated to turn out well'.

sentences.t:
Vin sínom
skal maðr vinr vera
ok gialda giǫf við giǫf;
hlátr við hlátri
skyli hǫlðar taka,
en lausung við lygi.
sentences.l:
To his friend
a man should be a friend
and repay gift with gift;
laughter with laughter
men should receive,
but falseness with lying.
sentences.i: A man should be a friend to his friend and repay gift with gift; meet laughter with laughter — but treachery with deceit.
sentences.n: gialda giǫf við giǫf 'pay gift for gift'. hlátr við hlátri … lausung við lygi 'laughter for laughter … falseness for lying' — repay in kind. hǫlðar 'freemen, men'. The reciprocity ethic runs through this whole section.

sentences.t:
Vin sínom
skal maðr vinr vera,
þeim ok þess vin;
en óvinar síns
skyli engi maðr
vinar vinr vera.
sentences.l:
To his friend
a man should be a friend,
to him and to his friend;
but of his enemy
no man should
be the friend's friend.
sentences.i: Be a friend to your friend, and to his friend too; but no one should befriend his enemy's friend.
sentences.n: þeim ok þess vin 'to him and to his (friend's) friend'. óvinar síns … vinar vinr 'the friend's friend of one's enemy'. Friendship — and enmity — carries one link further.

sentences.t:
Veitstu, ef þú vin átt,
þann er þú vel trúir,
ok vill þú af hánom gott geta:
geði skaltu við þann blanda
ok giǫfom skipta,
fara at finna opt.
sentences.l:
Know, if you have a friend,
one whom you trust well,
and you wish to get good from him:
you should blend minds with him
and exchange gifts,
go to visit him often.
sentences.i: If you have a friend you truly trust, and want good to come of it: open your mind to him, trade gifts, and go to see him often.
sentences.n: Veitstu 'know thou' (veizt + þú). geði … blanda 'blend minds, share thoughts'. giǫfom skipta 'exchange gifts'. fara at finna opt 'go to meet often'. Real friendship needs tending.

sentences.t:
Ef þú átt annan,
þannz þú illa trúir,
vildu af hánom þó gott geta:
fagrt skaltu við þann mæla,
en flátt hyggia
ok gialda lausung við lygi.
sentences.l:
If you have another,
one whom you trust ill,
yet you wish to get good from him:
fair shall you speak to him,
but think falsely,
and repay falseness with lying.
sentences.i: But if you have another whom you scarcely trust, and still want something out of him: speak him fair while thinking otherwise, and pay his treachery back with deceit.
sentences.n: The darker counterpart to st. 44. þannz = þann es 'him whom'. fagrt … mæla … flátt hyggia 'speak fair but think crooked'. Cold pragmatism toward the untrustworthy — the poem states it without moralizing.

sentences.t:
Þat er enn of þann,
er þú illa trúir
ok þér er grunr at hans geði:
hlæia skaltu við þeim
ok um hug mæla;
glík skolo giǫld giǫfom.
sentences.l:
It is yet about the one
whom you trust ill
and you suspect his disposition:
you shall laugh with him
and speak against your thought;
like should the repayments be to the gifts.
sentences.i: As for the man you don't trust and whose intentions you suspect: laugh along with him and speak against your true mind — repayment should match the gift.
sentences.n: Continues st. 45. grunr at hans geði 'suspicion of his disposition'. um hug mæla 'speak against one's own mind', i.e. dissemble. glík skolo giǫld giǫfom 'the repayments should be like the gifts' — meet deceit with deceit.

sentences.i: I was young once, and travelled all alone; then I lost my way — but I felt rich when I found another person: one man is another's joy.
sentences.n: A beloved, warm stanza. villr vega 'astray of the roads' (villr 'lost'; vega gen. pl. of vegr). þóttomz 'I thought myself' (middle). maðr er mannz gaman 'man is man's delight' — the poem's great line on companionship.

sentences.t:
Mildir, frœknir
menn batst lifa,
sialdan sút ala;
en ósniallr maðr
uggir hotvetna,
sýtir æ glǫggr við giǫfom.
sentences.l:
Generous, valiant
men live best,
seldom nurse sorrow;
but the cowardly man
dreads everything,
the niggard ever grieves at giving.
sentences.i: Open-handed, brave men live best and seldom nurse grief; but the coward fears everything, and the miser is always pained at parting with a gift.
sentences.n: Mildir, frœknir 'generous (and) bold'. sút ala 'nurse sorrow'. uggir hotvetna 'dreads everything'. sýtir … glǫggr við giǫfom 'the stingy man grieves at gifts'. Generosity and courage belong together.

sentences.t:
Váðir mínar
gaf ek velli at
tveim trémǫnnom:
rekkar þat þóttoz,
er þeir ript hǫfðo;
neiss er nøkkviðr halr.
sentences.l:
My garments
I gave in a field
to two wooden men:
warriors they seemed,
when they had cloth;
ashamed is a naked man.
sentences.i: Out in a field I once gave my clothes to two wooden men; they looked like warriors once they were dressed — a naked man is a figure of shame.
sentences.n: A vivid vignette. velli at 'out on a field'. trémǫnnom 'wooden men' (posts or figures). rekkar 'warriors, men'. ript 'cloth, garment'. neiss er nøkkviðr halr 'a naked man is disgraced' (neiss 'put to shame'; nøkkviðr 'naked'). Even dignity leans on a little clothing.

sentences.t:
Hrørnar þǫll,
sú er stendr þorpi á,
hlýrat henni bǫrkr né barr;
svá er maðr,
sá er mangi ann:
hvat skal hann lengi lifa?
sentences.l:
The pine withers,
which stands on a mound,
neither bark nor needle shelters it;
so is the man,
whom no one loves:
why should he live long?
sentences.i: A pine tree withers standing out on the bare mound, with no bark or needles to shield it; so it is with a man no one loves — why should he live long?
sentences.n: Another famous simile. þǫll 'young fir/pine'. þorpi (dat. of þorp) here an open hillock. hlýrat 'shelters not' (hlýr + -at); bǫrkr 'bark', barr 'needles'. mangi ann 'no one loves' (unna + gen.; mangi 'no one'). Loneliness pictured as bare exposure.

sentences.t:
Eldi heitari
brennr með illom vinom
friðr fimm daga;
en þá sloknar,
er inn sétti kømr,
ok versnar allr vinskapr.
sentences.l:
Hotter than fire
burns among bad friends
peace for five days;
but then it is quenched,
when the sixth comes,
and all the friendship worsens.
sentences.i: Among false friends, goodwill blazes hotter than fire for five days — then, on the sixth, it dies down and the whole friendship turns sour.
sentences.n: Eldi heitari 'hotter than fire' (dat. of comparison). friðr 'peace, goodwill'. sloknar 'is quenched'. inn sétti (dagr) 'the sixth (day)'. vinskapr 'friendship'. A caution against friendship kindled too fast.

sentences.t:
Mikit eitt
skala manni gefa;
opt kaupir sér í litlo lof:
með hálfom hleifi
ok með hǫllo keri
fekk ek mér félaga.
sentences.l:
One great thing only
one should not give a man;
often one buys praise with little:
with half a loaf
and with a tilted cup
I got myself a companion.
sentences.i: You don't need to give lavishly to win a man; small gifts often buy goodwill — with half a loaf and a tipped-up cup I have gained a friend.
sentences.n: Mikit eitt … skala gefa 'should not give only one big thing' — many small kindnesses beat one grand gift. í litlo 'for little'. hálfom hleifi 'half a loaf'; hǫllo keri 'a leaning/half-full cup'. fekk mér félaga 'won myself a comrade'.

sentences.t:
Lítilla sanda,
lítilla sæva,
lítil ero geð guma;
þvíat allir menn
urðot iafnspakir,
hálf er ǫld hvar.
sentences.i: Small are the sands, small the seas, and small the minds of men; for not everyone was made equally wise — everywhere the world is only half-and-half.
sentences.n: An enigmatic, much-quoted stanza. lítilla sanda … sæva 'of small sands and seas' (gen.). urðot iafnspakir 'did not become equally wise' (urðot = urðu + -at). hálf er ǫld hvar 'the world is half everywhere' — wisdom and folly are always mixed.

sentences.t:
Meðalsnotr
skyli manna hverr,
æva til snotr séi;
þeim er fyrða
fegrst at lifa,
er vel mart vito.
sentences.l:
Moderately wise
should each of men be,
never let him be too wise;
for those of men
it is fairest to live,
who know a good many things well.
sentences.i: Every man should be moderately wise — never too wise; life is pleasantest for those who know a fair amount, but not too much.
sentences.n: The first of three twinned stanzas on moderation in wisdom. Meðalsnotr 'middling-wise'. æva til snotr séi 'let him never be too wise' (séi, subjunctive of vera). fyrða 'of men' (gen. pl. of firar). vel mart 'a good deal'.

sentences.t:
Meðalsnotr
skyli manna hverr,
æva til snotr séi;
þvíat snotrs mannz hiarta
verðr sialdan glatt —
ef sá er alsnotr, er á.
sentences.l:
Moderately wise
should each of men be,
never let him be too wise;
for a wise man's heart
seldom becomes glad,
if he who owns it is all-wise.
sentences.i: Every man should be moderately wise, never too wise; for the heart of a wise man is seldom glad, if its owner is wise to a fault.
sentences.n: A twin of st. 54. snotrs mannz hiarta 'a wise man's heart' (gen.). glatt 'glad' (neut. of glaðr). alsnotr 'all-wise, too wise'. Too much wisdom breeds unhappiness.

sentences.t:
Meðalsnotr
skyli manna hverr,
æva til snotr séi;
ørlǫg sín
viti engi fyrir:
þeim er sorgalausastr sefi.
sentences.i: Every man should be moderately wise, never too wise; let no one foreknow his own fate — his mind will be the most free of sorrow.
sentences.n: The third twin. ørlǫg sín 'his fate' (ørlǫg, n. pl.). viti … fyrir 'know beforehand'. sorgalausastr sefi 'the most sorrow-free mind' (sefi 'mind, heart'). Not to know one's fate is a mercy.

sentences.t:
Brandr af brandi
brenn, unz brunninn er,
funi kveykiz af funa;
maðr af manni
verðr at máli kuðr,
en til dœlskr af dul.
sentences.l:
A brand from a brand
burns, until it is burnt out,
flame is kindled from flame;
a man from a man
becomes known by his speech,
but too foolish from seclusion.
sentences.i: One log takes fire from another until it is burned out, flame kindles flame; and a man is made known — and wiser — through talk with others, but grows foolish keeping to himself.
sentences.n: A fine image for learning through company (cf. st. 47). verðr at máli kuðr 'becomes known through speech' (kuðr 'known'). til dœlskr af dul 'too foolish from aloofness' (dœlskr 'silly'; dul 'concealment, keeping to oneself'). Isolation dulls a man.

sentences.t:
Ár skal rísa,
sá er annars vill
fé eða fiǫr hafa;
sialdan liggiandi úlfr
lær um getr,
né sofandi maðr sigr.
sentences.l:
Early should rise
he who wants to have
another's wealth or life;
seldom does a lying wolf
get a haunch,
nor a sleeping man victory.
sentences.i: A man who wants another's goods or blood must rise early; a wolf lying down rarely gets the meat, nor a sleeping man the victory.
sentences.n: Ár skal rísa 'one must rise early'. fé eða fiǫr 'property or life'. liggiandi úlfr 'a lying-down wolf'; lær 'ham, haunch of meat'. Fortune favours the early and active.

sentences.t:
Ár skal rísa,
sá er á yrkendr fá,
ok ganga síns verka á vit;
mart um dvelr,
þann er um morgin søfr,
hálfr er auðr und hvǫtom.
sentences.i: A man with few hands to help must rise early and see to his work; the one who sleeps late falls behind — the quick and active are already halfway to wealth.
sentences.n: Companion to st. 58 on diligence. á yrkendr fá 'has few labourers' (yrkendr 'workers'). síns verka á vit 'to attend to his work'. mart um dvelr 'much delays (him)'. hálfr er auðr und hvǫtom 'half the wealth is at the disposal of the energetic' (hvatr 'quick, bold').

sentences.t:
Þurra skíða
ok þakinna næfra,
þess kann maðr miǫt,
ok þess viðar,
er vinnaz megi
mál ok misseri.
sentences.l:
Of dry logs
and of roofing-bark,
of that a man knows the measure,
and of that timber
which may last
a term and a season.
sentences.i: A man can reckon how much dry firewood and roofing-bark he needs, and how much timber will see him through the seasons.
sentences.n: A homely stanza on husbandry. Þurra skíða 'of dry billets' (skíð 'split log'). þakinna næfra 'of roofing birch-bark' (næfr 'birch-bark'). miǫt 'measure, due amount'. vinnaz megi 'may suffice, last'. mál ok misseri 'a term and a half-year (season)'. Practical foresight.

glossary.alda: ǫld (f.) — age; (pl.) men, people; here gen. pl., 'of men' (alda sonom 'for the sons of men')
glossary.aldrtrega: aldrtregi (m.) — lifelong grief (aldr 'life' + tregi 'grief'); here acc. sg.
glossary.allar: allr — all; here nom./acc. pl. fem.
glossary.allt: allr — all, everything; here nom./acc. sg. neut.
glossary.annars: annarr — other, another; here gen. sg., 'of another' (annars brióstom 'in another's breast')
glossary.at: at — that (conj.); to (before an infinitive); at (prep., 'at, with'); also the suffixed negative -at on verbs
glossary.augabragði: augabragð (n.) — the twinkling of an eye; a mockery; at augabragði verðr 'becomes a laughing-stock' (dat. sg.)
glossary.augom: auga (n.) — eye; here dat. pl., 'with the eyes'
glossary.auði: auðr (m.) — wealth, riches; here dat. sg., 'than wealth' (auði betra)
glossary.bana: bani (m.) — death; slayer; here acc. sg., 'death' (bíðr bana 'awaits his death')
glossary.batst: beztr / batst — best (superlative, neut.); ǫlðr batst 'the best of ale-feasts'
glossary.berrat: bera — to bear, carry; berrat = berr + -at, '(he) bears not' (3rd sg. pres.)
glossary.betra: betri — better; here neut., 'better' (betra en 'better than')
glossary.brauto: braut (f.) — road, way; here dat. sg. (brauto at 'on the road')
glossary.brióstom: brióst (n.) — breast; here dat. pl. (annars brióstom í/ór 'in/out of another's breast')
glossary.brǫndom: brandr (m.) — firebrand, burning log; here dat. pl., 'at the fire-logs' (the hearth)
glossary.bíðr: bíða — to wait for, await (+ gen.); here 3rd sg. pres., 'awaits'
glossary.drekki: drekka — to drink; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'let him drink'
glossary.drekkr: drekka — to drink; here 3rd sg. pres., 'drinks'
glossary.dælt: dæll — easy, undemanding; here neut., 'easy' (dælt er heima hvat 'everything is easy at home')
glossary.einn: einn — one; alone; sá einn 'he alone'
glossary.ekki: ekki — nothing; not at all (neut. of engi)
glossary.elz: eldr (m.) — fire; here gen. sg. (elz er þǫrf 'there is need of fire')
glossary.endrþǫgo: endrþǫgn (f.) — silence in return, answering silence (endr 'again, in return' + þǫgn 'silence'); here gen. sg.
glossary.er: vera — to be (3rd sg. pres. 'is'); also the relative particle 'who, which, that'
glossary.etr: eta — to eat; here 3rd sg. pres., 'eats' (etr sér 'eats for himself')
glossary.eyrom: eyra (n.) — ear; here dat. pl., 'with the ears'
glossary.farit: fara — to go, travel; here past participle, 'travelled' (um fiall farit)
glossary.fialars: Fialarr (m.) — Fialar, a proper name (here gen. sg.); a figure linked to the mead of poetry
glossary.fiall: fiall (n.) — mountain, fell; here acc. sg. (um fiall farit 'travelled over the mountain')
glossary.fiǫlð: fiǫlð (f.) — a great deal, multitude; here acc., 'far and wide, much' (fiǫlð um farit)
glossary.fiǫtraðr: fiǫtra — to fetter, shackle; here past participle masc., 'fettered'
glossary.fiǫðrom: fiǫðr (f.) — feather; here dat. pl., 'with the feathers'
glossary.fleira: fleiri — more; here neut. comparative, 'the more'
glossary.fleti: flet (n.) — the raised plank-floor / benches of a hall; here dat. sg. (á fleti fyrir 'on the benches ahead')
glossary.frama: frami (m.) — advancement, distinction, worth; here gen. sg. (freista frama 'to test one's worth')
glossary.freista: freista (+ gen.) — to try, test, make trial of (infinitive)
glossary.frið: friðr (m.) — peace; here acc. sg. (engi frið 'no peace')
glossary.fróða: fróðr — wise, learned; here weak masc., 'the wise' (ins fróða Fialars 'of wise Fialar')
glossary.fróðra: fróðr — wise; here gen. pl. (fróðra hverr 'every wise man')
glossary.fugls: fugl (m.) — bird; here gen. sg. (þess fugls 'of that bird')
glossary.fyrir: fyrir — before, ahead, in front (adv./prep.); nýsiz fyrir 'scouts ahead'
glossary.fær: fá — to get, obtain; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gets'
glossary.færa: fár / færi — fewer, less; here neut. comparative, 'the less'
glossary.gangi: ganga — to go, walk; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, '(before) one goes'
glossary.gangir: ganga — to go; here 2nd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'that you go'
glossary.garði: garðr (m.) — enclosure, court, dwelling; here dat. sg. (í garði Gunnlaðar 'in Gunnlǫð's court')
glossary.gefendr: gefandi (m.) — giver; here nom. pl., 'the givers' (of hospitality), i.e. the hosts (pres. part. of gefa)
glossary.gefi: gefa — to give; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'may give' (þótt geirar gefi)
glossary.gefr: gefa — to give; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gives'
glossary.geta: geta — to get; here infinitive, 'to get' (gott geta 'to get benefit')
glossary.getr: geta — to get; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gets' (sér um getr 'gets for himself')
glossary.geði: geð (n.) — mind; here dat. sg. (at geði 'in mind'; hverio geði 'by what disposition')
glossary.geðs: geð (n.) — mind; here gen. sg. (geðs viti 'minds his sense'; til geðs 'of his mind')
glossary.gott: góðr — good; here neut., 'good' (gott geta 'to get good/benefit')
glossary.guma: gumi (m.) — man; here gen./acc. sg. (geð guma 'a man's mind')
glossary.gumna: gumi (m.) — man; here gen. pl. (gumna hverr 'every man')
glossary.gunnlaðar: Gunnlǫð (f.) — Gunnlǫð, the giantess who guarded the mead of poetry; here gen. sg.
glossary.gáttir: gátt (f.) — doorway, door-opening; here acc. pl., 'the doorways'
glossary.gætinn: gætinn — heedful, wary, cautious (nom. sg. masc.)
glossary.góðs: góðr — good; here gen. sg. neut. (góðs um œðis 'of a good disposition')
glossary.haldit: halda — to hold; haldit = haldi + -at, 'let (him) not hold on' (subjunctive + neg.); halda á keri 'cling to the cup'
glossary.hefir: hafa — to have; here 3rd sg. pres., 'has'
glossary.hegri: hegri (m.) — heron; Óminnis hegri 'the heron of forgetfulness' (nom. sg.)
glossary.heilir: heill — hale, whole, sound; as a greeting 'hail!'; here nom. pl. masc.
glossary.heimisgarða: heimisgarðr (m.) — homestead, home-enclosure; here acc. pl. (kømr heimisgarða til 'comes to a homestead')
glossary.heimskom: heimskr — foolish; here dat. sg. masc. (manni heimskom 'to the foolish man')
glossary.heimtir: heimta — to draw home, get back, reclaim; here 3rd sg. pres. (aptr of heimtir 'gets back')
glossary.heitir: heita — to be called; here 3rd sg. pres., 'is called'
glossary.hinn: hinn — that one; hinn er sæll 'he is fortunate, who…' (demonstrative)
glossary.hlióði: hlióð (n.) — hearing; silence; here dat. sg. (þunno hlióði þegir 'is silent with taut hearing')
glossary.hlýðir: hlýða — to listen; here 3rd sg. pres., 'listens'
glossary.hlœgis: hlœgi (n.) — laughter, ridicule; here gen. sg. (fær hlœgis 'earns ridicule')
glossary.horskom: horskr — wise, shrewd; here dat. pl. (með horskom 'among the wise')
glossary.hvat: hvat — what; anything (heima hvat 'anything at home')
glossary.hverio: hverr — each, every; here dat. sg. neut. (hverio geði 'by what/each disposition')
glossary.hverr: hverr — each, every; every one (nom. sg. masc.)
glossary.hyggiandi: hyggiandi (f.) — thought, understanding, judgement; here dat. sg. (at hyggiandi sinni 'of his understanding')
glossary.hyggz: hyggia — to think; here 3rd sg. pres. middle, 'thinks (to himself)' (hyggz muno lifa 'thinks he will live')
glossary.hánom: hann — he; here dat. sg., 'to him, for him'
glossary.hófi: hóf (n.) — moderation, measure; here dat. sg. (at hófi 'in moderation')
glossary.ill: illr — bad, ill; here nom. pl. neut. (ill ráð 'bad counsel')
glossary.inn: inn — in, inward (adv. of motion); also the article inn 'the' (inn vari gestr 'the wary guest')
glossary.ins: inn (article) — the; here gen. sg. masc. (ins fróða Fialars 'of wise Fialar')
glossary.kalinn: kala — to chill, freeze; here past participle masc., 'chilled, numb' (á kné kalinn 'frozen to the knee')
glossary.kann: kunna — to know, be able; here 3rd sg. pres., 'knows'
glossary.keri: ker (n.) — cup, vessel; here dat. sg. (halda á keri 'hold the cup')
glossary.kné: kné (n.) — knee; here acc. sg. (á kné kalinn 'chilled to the knee')
glossary.kominn: koma — to come; here past participle masc., 'come, arrived' (er kominn 'has come')
glossary.kveða: kveða — to say, declare; here 3rd pl. pres., 'they say' (sem gott kveða 'as it is called good')
glossary.kynnis: kynni (n.) — acquaintance, a visit; here gen. sg. (til kynnis kømr 'comes visiting')
glossary.kópir: kópa — to gape, stare stupidly; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gapes'

glossary.lifir: lifa — to live; here 3rd sg. pres., 'lives' (meðan lifir 'while he lives')
glossary.líknstafi: líknstafr (m.) — a token/word of favour, kindly regard; here acc. pl.
glossary.manni: maðr (m.) — man; here dat. sg., 'to/for a man'
glossary.matar: matr (m.) — food; here gen. sg. (matar þǫrf 'need of food')
glossary.mikit: mikill — great, much; here neut., 'much' (manvit mikit 'much good sense')
glossary.miǫk: miǫk — much, very, greatly (adv.); Neckel's spelling with ǫ (later Icelandic mjök)
glossary.miǫð: miǫðr (m.) — mead; here acc. sg. (drekki miǫð 'drink mead')
glossary.muno: munu — shall, will (auxiliary); here infinitive, 'will' (muno ey lifa 'will live forever')
glossary.mæli: mæla — to speak; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'let him speak'

glossary.nýsiz: nýsa — to peer, investigate; here 3rd sg. pres. middle, 'feels his way, scouts' (nýsiz fyrir)
glossary.of: of — an old expletive particle before verbs (of heimtir); also 'over, about'
glossary.ofdrykkia: ofdrykkia (f.) — over-drinking, excess in drink; here nom. sg. (ofdrykkia ǫls 'over-drinking of ale')
glossary.ofrǫlvi: ofrǫlvi — dead-drunk, over-drunk (of- intensive + ǫlvi 'drunk'); here nom. sg. masc.
glossary.orz: orð (n.) — word, speech; here gen. sg. (orz ok endrþǫgo 'of speech and silence-in-return')
glossary.ratar: rata — to travel, wander, roam; here 3rd sg. pres., 'travels' (víða ratar 'travels widely')
glossary.ráð: ráð (n.) — counsel, advice; here nom./acc. pl. (ill ráð 'bad counsel')
glossary.senn: senn — at once, all at once
glossary.sinn: sinn — his own (reflexive possessive); here acc. sg. (sinn bana 'his own death')
glossary.sinni: sinn — his own; here dat. sg. fem. (at hyggiandi sinni 'of his understanding')
glossary.sitia: sitja — to sit (infinitive); Neckel's spelling with i for j
glossary.sitr: sitja — to sit; here 3rd sg. pres., 'sits'
glossary.sitt: sinn — his own; here nom./acc. sg. neut. (sitt geð 'his own mind')
glossary.siá: siá — this, this one (demonstrative pron., nom. sg. masc.); Neckel's spelling of sjá
glossary.siálfr: siálfr — self, himself; here nom. sg. masc. (siálfr um á 'himself has')
glossary.skal: skulu — shall, must; here 3rd sg. pres., 'shall'
glossary.skoðar: skoða — to look at, examine; here 3rd sg. pres., 'looks about'
glossary.skoðaz: skoða — to look over; here with -z middle, 'be scanned' (um skoðaz skyli 'should be scanned')
glossary.skygnaz: skygnaz — to look about, peer around (a -z/-sk middle verb); here pres. subjunctive
glossary.skyli: skulu — shall, ought; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'should' (the gnomic 'one should')
glossary.slíkt: slíkr — such; here neut., 'such (is)' (slíkt er 'such is')
glossary.snotrom: snotr — wise, sensible; here dat. pl. (með snotrom 'among the wise')
glossary.sonom: sonr (m.) — son; here dat. pl. (alda sonom 'for the sons of men')
glossary.stað: staðr (m.) — place; here dat. sg. (í ókunnom stað 'in an unknown place')
glossary.stelr: stela (+ dat.) — to steal; here 3rd sg. pres., 'steals' (stelr geði 'steals the mind')
glossary.stýrir: stýra (+ dat.) — to steer, govern; here 3rd sg. pres., 'governs'
glossary.sylg: sylgr (m.) — a drink, draught; here acc. sg. (sylg um getr 'gets a drink')
glossary.sá: sá — that, that one; he (demonstrative pron., nom. sg. masc.); sá er 'he who'
glossary.sæll: sæll — fortunate, happy, blessed (nom. sg. masc.)
glossary.sé: vera — to be; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'be, may be' (þótt sé; en sé)
glossary.sér: sik — oneself; here dat. reflexive, 'for himself, to himself'
glossary.síns: sinn — his own; here gen. sg. (síns frama 'of his worth'; síns til geðs 'of his mind')
glossary.til: til — to, toward; (with gen.) of; til kynnis 'to a visit'; til geðs 'of his mind'
glossary.um: um — an old expletive particle before verbs (um getr, um á, um farit); also 'about, over, around'
glossary.uppi: uppi — up, out, exposed (adv.); uppi er geð 'the mind is laid bare'
glossary.varaz: varask (+ acc.) — to beware, guard against; here 3rd sg. pres. middle (við víg varaz 'guards against war')
glossary.vari: varr — wary, cautious; here weak masc., 'the wary' (inn vari gestr)
glossary.vark: vera — to be; here old 1st sg. past, 'I was' (ek fiǫtraðr vark)

glossary.vatz: vatn (n.) — water; here gen. sg. (vatz er þǫrf 'there is need of water')
glossary.vegnest: vegnest (n.) — journey-provisions, road-food (vegr 'way' + nest 'provisions'); here nom./acc. sg.

glossary.veit: vita — to know; here 3rd sg. pres., 'knows'
glossary.velli: vǫllr (m.) — field, open ground; here dat. sg. (velli at 'on the field')
glossary.verra: verri — worse; here neut. comparative (vegnest verra 'a worse provision')
glossary.verðar: verðr (m.) — meal; here gen. sg. (til verðar kømr 'comes to a meal')

glossary.vin: vinr (m.) — friend; here acc. sg. (óbrigðra vin 'a more faithful friend')
glossary.vitandi: vita — to know; here pres. participle, 'knowing' (vitandi vits 'having understanding of wit')
glossary.viti: vita — to know; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'has/minds' (geðs viti 'minds his sense')
glossary.vits: vit (n.) — wit, sense; here gen. sg. (vitandi vits 'knowing of wit')
glossary.vitz: vit (n.) — wit; here gen. sg. (vitz er þǫrf 'there is need of wit'); Neckel spells vitz
glossary.við: við — against; with; toward (prep.); við víg varaz 'guards against war'; ódælla er við þat 'harder with that'
glossary.válaðs: válaðr (m.) — a destitute, wretched man; here gen. sg. (válaðs vera 'the refuge of the destitute')
glossary.vár: vá — to blame, find fault, reproach; here 3rd sg. pres. (vár þik engi maðr 'no man will reproach you')
glossary.váða: váð (f.) — cloth, clothing; here gen. sg. (matar ok váða 'of food and clothing')
glossary.víg: víg (n.) — battle, war, killing; here acc. sg. (við víg varaz 'guards against war')
glossary.vígdiarft: vígdiarfr — bold in war; here neut., 'war-bold'
glossary.víti: víti (n.) — harm, penalty, mishap; here nom./acc. sg. (víti verðr 'harm befalls')
glossary.vǫrom: varr — wary; here dat. pl. (víti vǫrom 'harm to the wary')
glossary.yfir: yfir — over, above (prep.); yfir ǫlðrom þrumir 'broods over ale-feasts'
glossary.á: á — on, upon, at (prep.); also 'has, owns' (3rd sg. of eiga: siálfr um á 'himself has')
glossary.óbrigðra: óbrigðr — unfailing, faithful; here comparative, 'a more faithful' (óbrigðra vin)
glossary.ódælla: ódæll — difficult, hard to deal with; here comparative neut., 'harder' (ódælla er við þat)
glossary.ókunnom: ókunnr — unknown, unfamiliar; here dat. sg. (í ókunnom stað 'in an unknown place')
glossary.ókynnis: ókynni (n.) — discourtesy, bad manners; here gen. sg. (ókynnis vár 'blame for discourtesy')
glossary.óminnis: óminni (n.) — forgetfulness, oblivion; here gen. sg. (Óminnis hegri 'heron of forgetfulness')
glossary.ór: ór — out of, from (prep.); annars brióstom ór 'out of another's breast'
glossary.óvinir: óvinr (m.) — enemy, foe (ó- 'un-' + vinr 'friend'); here nom. pl.
glossary.óvíst: óvíss — uncertain; here neut., 'uncertain' (óvíst er at vita 'it is uncertain to know')
glossary.þarft: þarfr — needful, useful; here neut., 'what is needful' (mæli þarft 'speak what is useful')
glossary.þagalt: þagall — silent, reserved, discreet; here neut., 'discreet' (Þagalt ok hugalt)
glossary.þegi: þegja — to be silent; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'let him be silent'
glossary.þegir: þegja — to be silent; here 3rd sg. pres., 'is silent'
glossary.þegit: þiggja — to receive, accept; here past participle, 'received' (opt þegit 'often received')
glossary.þeim: sá / þeir — that one; them; here dat., 'to/for him' or 'to them'
glossary.þeims: þeim es — 'for him who' (þeim + relative particle es/er)
glossary.þerro: þerra (f.) — a towel, drying-cloth; here gen. sg. (þerro ok þióðlaðar)
glossary.þess: sá — that; here gen. sg., 'of that' (þess fugls 'of that bird'; ókynnis þess 'of this discourtesy')
glossary.þikkir: þykkja — to seem; here 3rd sg. pres., 'seems' (auði betra þikkir 'seems better than wealth')
glossary.þióðans: þióðann (m.) — king, prince, lord; here gen. sg. (þióðans barn 'a prince's child')
glossary.þióðlaðar: þióðlǫð (f.) — a hearty invitation, warm welcome; here gen. sg.
glossary.þrumir: þruma — to sit torpid, brood, hover; here 3rd sg. pres. (yfir ǫlðrom þrumir 'broods over ale-feasts')
glossary.þu: þú — you (2nd sg.); Neckel's spelling þu (unaccented)
glossary.þunno: þunnr — thin; here dat. sg. neut. (þunno hlióði 'with taut/strained hearing')
glossary.því: því — for, because (því at); therefore; (instrumental of sá) 'by that'
glossary.þvíat: þvíat — because, for (Neckel writes it as one word)
glossary.þylsk: þylja — to mutter, recite under one's breath; here 3rd sg. pres. middle, 'mumbles to himself'
glossary.þǫrf: þǫrf (f.) — need; er þǫrf (+ gen.) 'there is need of' (nom. sg.)
glossary.œðis: œði (n.) — disposition, temper, manner; here gen. sg. (góðs um œðis 'of good manner')
glossary.ǫlr: ǫlr — drunk, tipsy (nom. sg. masc.); ǫlr ek varð 'drunk I became'
glossary.ǫls: ǫl (n.) — ale; here gen. sg. (ofdrykkia ǫls 'over-drinking of ale')
glossary.ǫlðr: ǫlðr (n.) — ale-feast, drinking-bout; here nom. sg. (ǫlðr batst 'the best of ale-feasts')
glossary.ǫlðrom: ǫlðr (n.) — ale-feast; here dat. pl. (yfir ǫlðrom 'over ale-feasts')
glossary.aldar: ǫld (f.) — age; (pl.) men; here gen. sg. (aldar róg 'the strife of men')
glossary.aldir: ǫld (f.) — age; (pl.) men, people; here acc. pl. (með aldir 'among people')
glossary.alla: allr — all; here acc. pl. masc. (alla vera 'all to be')
glossary.annan: annarr — other, another; here acc. sg. masc.
glossary.biðia: biðja (+ gen.) — to ask, beg (infinitive); Neckel's spelling with i for j
glossary.blóðugt: blóðugr — bloody; here neut. (blóðugt hiarta 'a bleeding heart')
glossary.búi: búa — to dwell, live; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'though he dwell' (þótt á brauto búi)
glossary.bœn: bœn (f.) — a request, begging, prayer; here dat. sg. (betra en bœn 'better than begging')
glossary.eigi: eiga — to own; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'though he own' (þótt … eigi)
glossary.einom: einn — one; here dat. sg. (í einom stað 'in one place')
glossary.erosk: vera — 'are (to one another)' (ero + reciprocal -sk); erosk gagnhollir 'are mutually kind'
glossary.eyvito: eyvit — nothing at all; here acc., 'nothing' (eyvito leyna 'hide nothing')
glossary.fannka: finna — to find; fannka = fann + suffixed negative -ka, 'I found not' (1st sg. past)
glossary.farinn: fara — to go; here past participle masc., 'gone, travelled' (firr farinn 'gone farther')
glossary.fengit: fá — to get, win; here past participle, 'got, gained' (fengit hefr 'has got')
glossary.feti: fet (n.) — a pace, step; here dat. sg. (feti framarr 'a pace further')
glossary.finnr: finna — to find; here 3rd sg. pres., 'finds'
glossary.firar: firar (m. pl.) — men; here nom. pl. (hans freista firar 'men test him')
glossary.fiár: fé (n.) — cattle, property, wealth; here gen. sg. (fiár síns 'of his wealth')
glossary.fiðr: finna — to find, notice; here 3rd sg. pres. (hitki hann fiðr 'this he does not notice')
glossary.fletiom: flet (n.) — hall-benches; here dat. pl. (annars fletiom á 'on another's benches')
glossary.flótta: flótti (m.) — flight; here acc. sg. (flótta tekr 'takes to flight')
glossary.formælendr: formælandi (m.) — advocate, spokesman; here acc./nom. pl. (formælendr fá 'few advocates')
glossary.freginn: fregna — to ask; here past participle masc., 'questioned' (freginn erat 'is not questioned')
glossary.fá: fá — few; here nom./acc. pl. (formælendr fá 'few advocates'); also the verb fá 'to get'
glossary.fáa: fá — to get, take; here infinitive (verðar fáa 'take meals')
glossary.fár: fár (n.) — harm, malice, mischief; here acc. (fár lesi 'pick out malice')
glossary.féar: fé (n.) — property, wealth; here gen. sg. (síns féar 'of his wealth')
glossary.gagnhollir: gagnhollr — mutually well-disposed, friendly; here nom. pl. masc.
glossary.gagnvegir: gagnvegr (m.) — a direct way, short-cut; here nom. pl. (liggia gagnvegir 'short-cuts lie')
glossary.geirs: geirr (m.) — spear; here gen. sg. (geirs um þǫrf 'need of a spear')
glossary.geitr: geit (f.) — goat; here acc. pl. (tvær geitr 'two goats')
glossary.gelr: gala — to crow, chant, sing; here 3rd sg. pres. (sér ógott um gelr 'sings itself no good')
glossary.gengr: ganga — to go; here 3rd sg. pres. (mart gengr verr 'much goes worse')
glossary.gest: gestr (m.) — guest; here acc./dat. sg. (gestr at gest 'guest at guest')
glossary.glami: glamma — to make noise, chatter; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive (með grǫmom glami 'chatters among the hostile')
glossary.glissir: glissa — to grin, sneer; here 3rd sg. pres. (um verði glissir 'sneers over the meal')
glossary.grasi: gras (n.) — grass; here dat. sg. (ganga af grasi 'leave the grass')

glossary.vega: vegr (m.) — way, road; here gen. pl. (villr vega 'lost of the ways')
glossary.veitstu: vita — to know; veitstu = veizt + þú, 'do you know, know thou'
glossary.vel: vel — well (adv.); at verða vel 'to turn out well'; vel trúir 'trust well'
glossary.verka: verk (n.) — work, deed; here gen. sg. (síns verka 'of his work')
glossary.versnar: versna — to worsen, grow worse; here 3rd sg. pres. (versnar vinskapr 'the friendship sours')
glossary.verða: verða — to become; to turn out (infinitive); at verða vel 'to turn out well'
glossary.vildu: vilja — to will, want; vildu = vill + þú, 'you would/want' (vildu … geta)
glossary.vill: vilja — to will, want; here 2nd/3rd sg. pres. (sá er annars vill 'who wants another's')
glossary.villr: villr — astray, lost; here nom. sg. masc. (villr vega 'lost of the ways')
glossary.vinir: vinr (m.) — friend; here nom. pl. (vinir gleðiaz 'friends gladden each other')
glossary.vinnaz: vinna — to work; to last, suffice; here infinitive middle (er vinnaz megi 'which may last')
glossary.vinom: vinr (m.) — friend; here dat. pl. (illom vinom 'bad friends')
glossary.viðar: viðr (m.) — wood, timber; here gen. sg. (þess viðar 'of that timber')
glossary.viðrgefendr: viðrgefandi (m.) — one who gives in return; here nom. pl. (pres. part.)
glossary.váðir: váð (f.) — cloth, garment; here nom./acc. pl. (Váðir mínar 'my clothes')
glossary.váðom: váð (f.) — cloth, garment; here dat. pl. (vápnom ok váðom 'with weapons and clothes')
glossary.yrkendr: yrkjandi (m.) — a worker, labourer; here acc. pl. (á yrkendr fá 'has few workers')
glossary.ár: ár — early (adv.); ár skal rísa 'should rise early'
glossary.átt: eiga — to own, have; here 2nd sg. pres., 'you have' (ef þú vin átt)
glossary.óvinar: óvinr (m.) — enemy; here gen. sg. (óvinar síns 'of his enemy')
glossary.ørlǫg: ørlǫg (n. pl.) — fate, destiny; here acc. (ørlǫg sín 'his fate')
glossary.þakinna: þakinn — covering, for roofing (past part. of þekja 'to thatch'); here gen. pl. (þakinna næfra 'of roofing-bark')
glossary.þann: sá — that one; him; here acc. sg. masc. (við þann 'to him'; of þann 'about that one')
glossary.þannz: þann es — 'him whom' (þann + relative particle es/er); þannz þú illa trúir
glossary.þorpi: þorp (n.) — an open hillock, exposed spot; a hamlet; here dat. sg. (þorpi á 'on a mound')
glossary.þurra: þurr — dry; here gen. pl. (Þurra skíða 'of dry logs')
glossary.þér: þú — you; here dat. sg., 'to you' (þér er grunr 'you have suspicion')
glossary.þóttomz: þykkja — to seem; here 1st sg. past middle, 'I thought myself' (auðigr þóttomz)
glossary.þóttoz: þykkja — to seem; here 3rd pl. past middle, 'they seemed/thought themselves' (rekkar þóttoz)
glossary.þǫll: þǫll (f.) — a young fir, pine (nom. sg.)
glossary.ǫld: ǫld (f.) — age; the world; men; here nom. sg. (hálf er ǫld 'the world is half')
